title: Chirped-pulse engineering for robust control of single-molecule orientation in a cavity
authors: Li-Bao Fan, Yu Guo, Shan Ma, Chuan-Cun Shu
abstract: We present a theoretical investigation of coherent control over the orientation of an individual molecule strongly coupled with a cavity using chirped-pulse driving. Specifically, we explore the dynamics of carbonyl sulfide (OCS) molecules under the influence of two chirped pulses with different spectral phases. We compare two pulse configurations: one with equal chirp rates ($β_{+} = β_{-}$) and another with unequal chirp rates ($β_{+} \neq β_{-}$). Numerical simulations reveal that chirped pulses enable precise control of the molecular orientation, achieving a maximum orientation degree of 0.5773. By analyzing the distribution of molecular polariton states, we show that chirped pulses can activate multiphoton processes, leading to deviations from the predictions of first-order Magnus expansion methods. Additionally, we demonstrate the robustness of the maximum orientation with respect to chirp amplitude and detuning, providing insights into the role of pulse parameters in optimizing control. This work introduces a new strategy for controlling molecular orientation in cavity-based systems and offers valuable perspectives for future experimental applications.
summary: This paper investigates how chirped laser pulses can be used to control the orientation of a single molecule inside an optical cavity. It shows that by engineering the chirp rates, one can achieve high degrees of orientation and robust control that is resistant to frequency detuning. This work offers a new strategy for manipulating molecular polaritons in cavity-based quantum systems.
main_problem: Achieving robust and precise coherent control over the orientation of a single molecule within an optical cavity using chirped-pulse engineering.
main_result: The study demonstrates that chirped pulses can precisely control molecular orientation, reaching a maximum degree of 0.5773, and that equal-chirp configurations provide superior robustness against frequency detuning.
method: The authors use numerical integration of the time-dependent wave function and compare results with the first-order Magnus expansion to analyze population redistribution and multiphoton processes.
model_or_system: A single carbonyl sulfide (OCS) molecule strongly coupled to a single-mode optical cavity, modeled using an extended Jaynes-Cummings model incorporating rotational states and a three-level polariton system.
key_observables: The degree of molecular orientation <cos theta> and the final populations of the polariton states.
important_parameters: Chirp rates (beta plus and beta minus), pulse amplitude (A), pulse detuning (delta), and the strong coupling regime (g0/omega_c <= 0.1).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the ability to achieve photon blockade of higher dressed states to isolate a three-state system and neglects quadratic terms like cavity-field self-energy.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows energy level diagrams in bare and dressed bases; Fig 2 shows orientation dependence on pulse amplitude and chirp; Fig 4 illustrates the orientation landscape and symmetry; Fig 5 compares numerical results to Magnus expansion; Fig 6 maps orientation robustness against detuning.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and model, presents the pulse engineering method, analyzes the results in weak and strong field regimes, evaluates the breakdown of analytical approximations, and concludes with a robustness analysis.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into using spectral-phase modulation to control molecular polaritons and demonstrates how chirped pulses can activate multiphoton processes that deviate from standard analytical approximations in cavity QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of a single molecule in a single-mode cavity driven by two chirped pulses (b), and the corresponding en- ergy level diagrams in the bare eigenbasis (a) and the dressed basis (c).

FIG. 2. Numerically calculated post-pulse orientation of the molec- ular polariton driven by chirped laser pulses with equal chirp rates β+ = β−. (a) The maximum orientation values |⟨cos θ⟩|max are shown vs the amplitude A± and the chirp rates β+ = β−. Cuts of |⟨cos θ⟩|max versus β+ = β−at three representative amplitudes: (b) A± = 0.1π, (c) A± = π, and (d) A± = 1.75π. Other parameters used in the numerical calculations are given in atomic units (a.u.): ω−= 1.66379 × 10−6 a.u., and ω+ = 2.03353 × 10−6 a.u.. The pulse duration τ0 = 5.409 × 108 a.u. ≈13.0851 ns, and the time interval is t ∈[−28τ0, 28τ0] ≈[−366.383, 366.383] ns.

FIG. 3. The final populations (a)-(c) and phases (d)-(f) of the three states |0; 0⟩, |±; 0⟩vs the chirp rate of the pusle at three different amplitudes A = 0.1π, A = π, and A = 1.75π. In panels (d)–(f), Ψ+ and Ψ−denote the phases of the states |+; 0⟩and |−; 0⟩, respectively, and ∆ψ is their phase difference. Note that the phases for larger detunings, where the populations of the states |+; 0⟩and |−; 0⟩are nearly zero, are meaningless and therefore are set to zero in our simulations.

FIG. 4. The maximum orientation values |⟨cos θ⟩|max are shown vs two chirp rates β+ and β−of the pulses at three different amplitudes (a) A = 0.1π, (b) A = π, and (c) A = 1.75π. (e-f) Lower panels show cuts of |⟨cos θ⟩|max versus β−at fixed β+, with β+ = 0 (black solid), β+ = +500 ns2 (red dashed), and β+ = −500 ns2 (blue-dotted), corresponding to (a-c), respectively. All other parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Final populations of the three polariton states versus the chirp rate β−for unequal-chirp driving, comparing exact numerical results with the first-order Magnus approximation. Panels (a)-(c), (d)-(f), and (h)-(j) correspond to three different amplitudes A± = 0.1π, π, and 1.75π, respectively. In each column, β+ is fixed at (a,d,h) 500 ns2, (b,e,i) 0, and (c,f,j) −500 ns2, while β−is scanned. Solid curves show the exact final populations of |0; 0⟩(black), |−; 0⟩(blue), and |+; 0⟩(red); filled circles indicate the corresponding first-order Magnus predictions. All other parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.
